reviews.author: Anonymous
reviews.review_body:
Most of the staff are only there because they had no better options. Teaching was a last resort for them, not a passion. As a result, they’re bitter, unprofessional, uninterested, and completely incapable of inspiring or shaping young minds. They go through the motions without any real effort, treating students like a burden instead of a responsibility.

Only a handful of students who show early signs of success are given any attention—just enough to create the illusion that the system works. Everyone else is ignored, left to rot in mediocrity with no real support or encouragement. Mistakes aren’t treated as part of learning; they’re punished or brushed aside, killing curiosity and growth.

The staff still carry a deep colonial hangover, blindly enforcing rules without logic or explanation. They don’t teach students how to think—just what to memorize. It’s less education, more indoctrination. They expect obedience, not understanding. The result? A generation of students who are either broken down or forced to conform, with no space to question, explore, or become anything more than what the system allows.

And the cruelty isn’t just systemic—it’s personal. One school principal reportedly made a horrifying comment suggesting that students who failed exams might as well kill themselves. Let that sink in: a person entrusted with young lives, casually throwing around such a statement. Senior teachers carry themselves with absurd, baseless arrogance, often reminding students that they're "dispensable like tissues" and can be "kicked out whenever we want." That’s not discipline—it’s abuse.

Slapping students, insulting them in front of peers, breaking them down emotionally—these are not isolated incidents. They’re common practice. And worst of all, it’s all normalized, ignored, or brushed under the rug by the very people who should be protecting students.

This isn’t just poor education. It’s institutionalized neglect and psychological harm—disguised as schooling.
